lenses.guidance: Hebrew chattat (vv 3, 14, 23, 28) carries both 'sin' and 'sin offering' — the same word names the problem and the remedy. The lexical doubling preaches: the offering takes the sinner's name on itself.

Blood applied to the horns of the altar (vv 7, 18, 25, 30, 34) — the pattern is contact-atonement: where the worshipper meets God, the blood meets first. Fulfilled at the cross where Christ's blood opens approach.

vv 13-21 cover unintentional sin even of the whole congregation — none too small, none too corporate. 1 Jn 2:2 lifts that scope to its limit: Jesus is 'the propitiation… for the sins of the whole world.'
vv 2, 13, 22, 27 — the unintentional sin still requires a sacrifice. Today the pattern teaches: ignorance is not innocence; we draw near in confession even of what we did not see at the time.
